critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is halting development and production of its electric vehicle (EV) models, including the electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and the GM-built Prologue. The author argues this decision will cause Honda to fall behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, potentially harming its future competitiveness.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of several EV models, including the electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the Prologue.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Shelving EV programs risks Honda falling behind in key industry trends: electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Honda's approach treats EVs as just cars with different drivetrains, which is a flawed strategy compared to designing EVs from the ground up.
The company will miss out on learning opportunities in EV development, manufacturing, supply chains, and customer feedback.
Honda is also behind in software-defined vehicle technology, which is increasingly important in the EV market.
Honda faces an identity crisis as a traditional internal combustion engine company while the market shifts toward EVs and autonomous features.
Honda's competitiveness is already declining in China due to inability to offer value compared to newer EV manufacturers, contributing to significant losses.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely consistent with publicly available information as of early 2026. Reports from Automotive News and other industry sources confirm Honda's halting of specific EV projects like the electric Acura RDX and the Prologue. Honda's challenges in competing with Chinese EV makers and the impact of tariffs are well-documented issues. The analysis of Honda's strategic shortcomings and risks in missing out on EV and SDV trends aligns with expert commentary in the automotive industry. However, the article is opinionated and speculative in parts, especially regarding Honda's future prospects and identity crisis, which are interpretations rather than confirmed facts.
considerations: The article does not include direct statements or detailed responses from Honda beyond general references to company blame on tariffs and competition.
It does not explore Honda's broader corporate strategy or potential plans for EVs beyond the canceled models.
The impact of global supply chain issues and semiconductor shortages on Honda's decisions is not discussed.
The article focuses primarily on the U.S. and Chinese markets without detailed analysis of Honda's position in other regions.
Potential partnerships or technological developments Honda might pursue in the EV or SDV space are not considered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to halt its EV development, with many commenters expressing concern that Honda risks falling behind in the rapidly evolving automotive industry dominated by EVs and software-defined vehicles. Opinions vary from criticism of Honda's strategy and skepticism about software-defined vehicles to cautious optimism about other automakers' approaches and reflections on market and infrastructure challenges.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the importance of software-defined vehicles in the automotive industry's future and critiques the business model of disabling features to charge fees, which is a valid concern.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: Provides a real-world example of EV adoption, supporting the argument that ICE vehicles have limited future in certain markets, reinforcing the urgency for automakers to shift focus.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the financial risks legacy automakers face if they fail to adapt to EVs, though the timeline may be somewhat speculative.

comments.author: nonford150
comments.text:
I almost pulled the trigger on a Prologue; so glad I had second thoughts. Even though it was essentially a GM product, I've only ever owned Hondas, so I thought "Well, at least I can get service at my Honda dealer".

Charging in the US (other than at home) is still the biggest issue for me. I do lots of traveling, and waiting 30-45 minutes to charge even at a Level 3 charger is a PITA. If I had a J std charger, then it's even longer. This makes my monthly 8 hour trips one-way another 2 hours - this sucks. Sorry - I'll keep my 2005 Honda Element with 445K miles. Another engine would be cheaper than less than a year of car payments. And it's pretty much indestructible.
comments.analysis: Highlights a practical barrier to EV adoption in the US, emphasizing infrastructure challenges that affect consumer decisions.

comments.author: alliao
comments.text: To be honest, I have every faith in Honda. It took them a long time to arrive at hybrid, but they were never about first to market, but they were always adamant about controlling the entire technology stack.. made their own transmission and everything. And engineering doesn't faze them, Honda just nonchalantly displayed a reusable rocket like it was too easy... EV is a little bit like AI nowadays, not much moat and possibly not challenging enough for Honda R&D so why not. I'll always be on the look out for Honda's next take on EV.
comments.analysis: Offers a nuanced perspective on Honda's strategy and engineering culture, suggesting patience and long-term planning rather than immediate abandonment.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
This is so unfortunate. I was never a van guy, but my wife insisted we get a van, so I got the Honda. And honestly? I kinda love it. It drives like a car but holds eight people (or four people and a whole bunch of luggage).

The way we use the van, 90% of our drives are under 20 miles round trip. The rest are longer road trips. I've been waiting eight years for Honda to make an electric or even a plug-in hybrid where the gas motor just charge the battery.

It would be perfect for my family. I guess that's not happening now.
comments.analysis: Personal anecdote illustrating consumer disappointment due to Honda's EV strategy, reflecting real user impact.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text:
> Consumers, mostly those who buy EVs from the likes of Tesla, Rivian, and BYD, have grown accustomed to the frequent updates, slick infotainment software, and advanced driver-assistance systems.

Guess which three items out of that list I do not want.
comments.analysis: Accurately summarizes consumer expectations in the EV market, highlighting software and tech features as key differentiators.

comments.author: troyvit
comments.text:
> By shelving EVs, Honda will fall farther behind in two of the biggest shifts sweeping the automotive industry: electric drivetrains [...]

Ugh that sucks

> [...] and software-defined vehicles.

Take my money! I'll suffer with gas for that.
comments.analysis: Concise expression of disappointment with Honda's decision, reinforcing the importance of EV and software trends.

comments.author: rob74
comments.text:
> When developed as an original product, EVs offer automakers a chance to rethink the automobile, and in the process, make it cheaper.

That does not bode well for German car makers either I'm afraid. Take BMW for instance: they started off with two "pure" EV models, the i3 (a compact car) and the i8 (a sports car). Both of them promising, but neither a particular bestseller. So they switched to offering electric drive as an alternative to IC engines in several (most?) "regular" models. But I agree with TechCrunch that this is more of a cop-out than a winning strategy...

> Consumers, mostly those who buy EVs from the likes of Tesla, Rivian, and BYD, have grown accustomed to the frequent updates, slick infotainment software, and advanced driver-assistance systems. Honda has yet to make significant progress in any of those domains.

Here's an idea: what about making an EV free from this enshittification? One where you can decide yourself when to install an update, like in the "olden days" a few years ago? One that doesn't pretend to have an "autopilot" which isn't really one? I think there would be a market for such an EV.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid consumer desire for simpler, user-controlled software updates, critiquing current trends toward forced updates and overcomplexity.

comments.author: ggm-at-algebras
comments.text:
In Shenzhen for a tech meeting. The streetscape is quieter, despite high traffic levels and I hear not only MORE birdsong, but the birds do more complex songlines.

The air is clean. For sure some of this is because it's a coastal city and has fresh sea breezes, but I've been in other Chinese coastal cities in times past and the air was significantly less clean.

There are social upsides for an almost-all-EV city.

This is an 18m person city. It's not exclusively wealthy people, its just a city with a very high local EV population and it shows.
comments.analysis: Provides observational evidence of environmental and social benefits in a heavily EV-adopted city, supporting the positive impact of EVs.
